h2.note: transcript-level proxy for the per-turn H2 (§4.3); needs a real (non-mock) full-N run to mean anything

cliIsolation.claude: non-persistent temp cwd with project/local settings and tools disabled

scored.recoheredEarlier: So the line goes here after three quarters; the 4 is not a speed mark, right?
scored.statedInsightEvidence: The 4 is under this one quarter-note beat unit, not the row.
scored.naiveTrajectory: Having placed the pencil after the third tile in L2, the learner was expected to simply draw the bar line on the next prompt.
scored.actionalBreakthroughEvidence: slides the window tight around the single quarter tile and draws a short underline beneath it
scored.actionalBreakthroughJustification: L4 physically enacts the new gate criterion introduced in T3 — isolating the single tile with the window and marking the underline — performing a new representational device rather than merely asserting understanding.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: The cards settled the count; they did not settle the checkpoint reader problem. Use this gate: through the window, the bottom 4 must attach to one quarter tile only, not the row. If that is true, draw the short underline under that one beat unit, then mark the bar line after the third quarter.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: T3 explicitly names the old route as insufficient ('cards settled the count; they did not settle the checkpoint reader problem') and introduces a new physical device — the blank-card window — that reframes the task as a spatial-attachment test, satisfying both the stock-taking contrast and the new public mechanism requirements.
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityEvidence: Use this gate: through the window, the bottom 4 must attach to one quarter tile only, not the row. If that is true, draw the short underline under that one beat unit, then mark the bar line after the third quarter.
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityJustification: The window gate is precisely fitted to the learner's stated blockage (label appearing to belong to the whole row), gives an actionable binary criterion (attaches to one tile or not), and requires the learner to perform the check rather than accept a teacher demonstration, though it is somewhat over-directed in specifying the exact sequence of steps.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: Top number check: three beats in one bar. Bottom number check: this underlined one beat unit. Draw the bar line after the third quarter and hand it in.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: T4 incorporates the underline produced by L4 into its closing checklist, acknowledging the learner's enacted action, but it largely continues the established lesson plan rather than visibly changing task or framing in response to the learner's revised frame.

scored.statedInsightEvidence: I think it is because `B` and `3` tell the place on the grid
scored.naiveTrajectory: After correctly predicting C4 as the address in L2, the learner was on track to complete the full worksheet line by also supplying the value.
scored.actionalBreakthroughEvidence: Address box: `C4`, because that is column `C` and row `4`. Value box: I'm leaving it blank for now, because I still do not see the number inside that cell. Is that right for this line?
scored.actionalBreakthroughJustification: The learner physically marks the worksheet, correctly applies the evidence-gate criterion introduced by the tutor (filling address, leaving value blank pending visible cell data), performing a new procedural standard rather than merely asserting understanding.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: Column plus row settles only the address box; the value box needs visible evidence from inside the cell. Use an evidence gate on this line: mark `address` as "column + row" and fill `C4`; mark `value` as "visible cell only" and leave it blank until `C4` is on screen. Then point to `C4` and copy the number showing inside that cell.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: Facing L3's stall (the value is unavailable), the tutor abandons the fill-both-boxes-now route and introduces a named evidence-gate device with explicit criteria labels for each box, giving the learner a new public standard rather than simply repeating the prior instruction.
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityEvidence: Use an evidence gate on this line: mark `address` as "column + row" and fill `C4`; mark `value` as "visible cell only" and leave it blank until `C4` is on screen.
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityJustification: The device is precisely fitted to the learner's blockage (no visible number), names distinct evidence standards for each box, and gives the learner an actionable criterion they can apply independently; it is somewhat over-directed in that the tutor pre-labels both boxes, limiting learner agency slightly.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: There is no tutor turn after the pivot turn L3 that responds to a revised learner framing; T3 precedes L4 and the transcript ends after L4, so no post-pivot tutor uptake is visible on the page.

scored.recoheredEarlier: the pencil mark underneath is pulling my eye the other way. Which mark counts for placement here: the plastic arrow or the diagram mark?
scored.statedInsightEvidence: The pencil mark is the cart's travel mark, so it does not receive the force tile.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner was heading toward resolving the competing-marks question by getting an authoritative ruling on which mark to use, then applying the same rule to push.
scored.actionalBreakthroughEvidence: sets the friction tile on the left visible plastic arrow, then lifts the strip without moving it. 'Friction stays on this left plastic arrow: the surface acts on the cart that way. The pencil mark is the cart's travel mark, so it does not receive the force tile. For push, the tile has to go on the plastic arrow for the hand's force, not just where the cart is moving.'
scored.actionalBreakthroughJustification: The learner physically executes the new gate criterion (tile on visible plastic arrow only), then independently applies the travel-mark/force-mark distinction to push without being prompted, showing the device has transferred.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: The old rule is not settling this because two marks are competing. Use this gate first: only marks visible above the strip can receive force labels. Put the friction tile on the visible arrow that qualifies. Then lift the strip and name the pencil mark's job without moving the tile.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: T3 explicitly names the failure of the prior route ('The old rule is not settling this because two marks are competing') and introduces a physically new device — the paper strip as a visibility gate — that changes the learner's task from choosing between two marks to working only with what the gate exposes, satisfying both the stock-taking contrast and the new public mechanism requirements.
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityEvidence: The old rule is not settling this because two marks are competing. Use this gate first: only marks visible above the strip can receive force labels. Put the friction tile on the visible arrow that qualifies. Then lift the strip and name the pencil mark's job without moving the tile.
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityJustification: The strip gate is precisely fitted to the learner's actual blockage (perceptual competition between two marks), is physically actionable and distinct from the prior verbal rule, and the two-step sequence (place under gate, then lift and classify) gives the learner an independent criterion for distinguishing force marks from travel marks rather than just receiving an answer.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: There is no tutor turn after the pivot learner turn L3, so no recognition-contingent uptake is available to score.
